planting.note: Plant container-grown or rooted cuttings in spring; in zone 9 plant year-round, with spring and fall preferred. Majorca Pink rosemary thrives in zone 9 and can be grown as an evergreen perennial shrub. It requires excellent drainage — water pooling around roots is the main cause of failure. Once established it is very drought-tolerant and requires minimal supplemental irrigation. The pink flowers are particularly showy from February through April in zone 9.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil — excellent drainage is more important than soil fertility for rosemary; Majorca Pink is a named cultivar of culinary rosemary distinguished by its delicate pink flowers in contrast to the blue-purple blooms of most rosemary varieties, making it equally valuable as an ornamental flowering shrub and a culinary herb. The upright to slightly spreading habit and profuse pink spring bloom (February–April in zone 9) create a spectacular display in herb gardens, Mediterranean-style landscapes, and edible ornamental borders. The foliage flavor and aroma of Majorca Pink is equivalent to standard rosemary — potent, resinous, piney, with notes of camphor — and it can be used in all the same culinary applications: roasting meats and vegetables, infused oils, herb breads, and as a fresh garnish. In zone 9 Majorca Pink is particularly drought-tolerant and heat-adapted, thriving in the intense summer sun that would stress less robust varieties.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Rosemary Cultivar Selection
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Rosemary for Texas Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Ornamental Herbs for Gulf South

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–April for best establishment. German Winter thyme is best planted in fall in zone 9 so it establishes through the mild winter before facing summer heat. Provide excellent drainage — thyme is far more likely to die from wet roots than from cold or heat. Mulch lightly to maintain even soil moisture in summer.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, sandy or gritty, lean soil — thyme rots in wet, heavy soils; German Winter is a named thyme cultivar selected for maximum cold hardiness among culinary thymes while retaining excellent flavor — the selection criterion being survival in harsh northern winters without the flavor reduction that sometimes accompanies cold-hardy breeding in other thyme types. The narrow, dark green, intensely aromatic leaves have a classic culinary thyme flavor — warm, slightly sweet, slightly piney, with clove-like notes — that is considered superior to many named thyme cultivars for cooking. German Winter forms a tidy, compact mound and remains evergreen in mild climates. It is the preferred culinary thyme for zone 4–7 gardeners where standard English thyme may winterkill. In zone 9 German Winter performs as a reliable perennial through its first several years before the heat-stressed plants may need replacement; fall planting ensures the best establishment.
sources: Purdue University Consumer Horticulture — German Winter Thyme
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Culinary Thyme Varieties
University of Georgia Extension — Thyme for Southern Gardens

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring; in zone 9 plant September–April. Hi Ho Silver creeping thyme makes excellent groundcover for dry, sunny slopes, rock gardens, and stepping stone pathways where foot traffic does not crush the low-growing stems. The silver-edged variegated foliage and pink-lavender bloom in spring create a tapestry effect when mass-planted. Provide very good drainage — standing water kills creeping thyme quickly.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy to rocky, lean soil — creeping thyme fails in wet or heavy soils; Hi Ho Silver is a variegated creeping thyme (Thymus praecox) with silver-margined leaves that create a two-tone silver-and-green appearance across the mat-forming plant, making it one of the most ornamentally attractive thymes available for groundcover use. The tiny, silver-edged leaves are fragrant when brushed or crushed and are mildly culinary — suitable as a garnish and for light flavoring, though not as potently flavored as upright culinary thyme varieties. In spring (March–April in zone 9) the entire mat is covered with small pink-lavender flowers that are highly attractive to bees and beneficial insects. Hi Ho Silver is widely used in rock gardens, between stepping stones where foot traffic releases the fragrance, as an edging plant, and as a fragrant groundcover in herb gardens. The prostrate, spreading habit fills in gaps efficiently without becoming invasive in well-drained soils.
sources: Penn State Extension — Ornamental Thyme Varieties
University of Minnesota Extension — Creeping Thyme Cultivars
Fine Gardening Magazine — Thymus praecox Hi Ho Silver

planting.note: Start from cuttings or transplants; in zone 9 plant March–May. Honey Stevia flowers profusely in September–October when days shorten — harvest leaves for drying before flowering begins, as the sweetness is highest in pre-flower leaves. In zone 9 the plant is a short-lived perennial, often surviving 2–3 years before requiring replacement. Provide light shade during the hottest summer weeks to prevent leaf scorch and premature bolting.

directions: Plant in full sun to light afternoon shade in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil; Honey Stevia is a named cultivar of stevia (Stevia rebaudiana) selected for a smoother, more honey-like sweetness compared to the standard species, which can have a distinct licorice-menthol aftertaste that some people find unpleasant in large quantities. The sweetening compounds in stevia (steviosides and rebaudiosides) are 200–350 times sweeter than sugar by weight, so only small amounts of fresh or dried leaf are needed to sweeten foods and beverages. The Honey cultivar has been selected to emphasize the rebaudioside-A fraction (which provides the cleaner, honey-like sweetness) relative to the stevioside fraction (associated with the bitter aftertaste) — making it the preferred variety for fresh leaf use in teas, lemonades, and fresh preparations where subtle sweetener differences are most noticeable. Leaves are used fresh in drinks and food, or dried and powdered for longer storage.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Stevia rebaudiana Cultivar Notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Stevia for Florida Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Stevia Production Guide

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed. In zone 9 start in January–February and transplant February–March for spring production. Support the large, indeterminate vines with cages or stakes. In zone 9 the fall planting in August–September often produces the highest-quality fruits as temperatures moderate; spring crops should be harvested before peak summer heat reduces fruit quality.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil with sturdy stake or cage support; Akers West Virginia is an heirloom tomato preserved in the Appalachian tradition of West Virginia seed saving, producing large, meaty, deep red beefsteak-type fruits with a classic, complex, sweet-acidic flavor typical of the best Old World tomato varieties maintained by Appalachian farm families for generations. The large (often 12–24 ounce) fruits have thick, meaty walls with small seed cavities and outstanding fresh-eating flavor — the balanced combination of sweetness and bright acidity makes Akers West Virginia one of the most satisfying large heirloom varieties for fresh slicing. The fruit shows good resistance to cracking despite its size. Like most Appalachian heirlooms, Akers West Virginia developed in a region with variable, challenging weather and has proven adaptability to different growing conditions. In zone 9 the fall crop (planted in August, harvested October–November) often produces exceptionally flavored fruits as cooler temperatures concentrate sugars and acid.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Akers West Virginia Tomato
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Appalachian Heirloom Tomatoes
West Virginia University Extension — Heirloom Tomato Varieties
